← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Mesopotamia & Persia

Cradles of civilization — Sumer, Assyria, Babylon, and the Achaemenids.

Between the Tigris and Euphrates, writing, law codes, and the first cities. Further east, the Persian empire spread a visual language of winged kings and processional staircases from Egypt to the Indus.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Tell Brak
Uruk-period urban center · c. 4000 BCE · northeast Syria
One of the world's earliest cities, older than Uruk itself, dominated by the Eye Temple and thousands of small alabaster 'eye idols' — figurines reduced to enormous staring pupils. A mass grave nearby suggests urban life here could end violently.
Mesopotamian Cylinder Seals
Sumerian onward · 4th millennium BCE onward
Tiny carved stone cylinders rolled across clay to sign a document. Each person owned one — an identity card and an art form at once.
Amulet Seal in the Form of a Bull
Mesopotamia, Sumerian, Iraq, Early Bronze Age I (c. 3300–3000 BCE) · c. 3250 BCE · limestone with black stone inlaid eyes
Warka Vase
Uruk period · c. 3200 BCE · Sumer
One of the earliest known narrative reliefs — a procession to the goddess Inanna. Looted from the Iraq Museum in 2003 and later recovered, broken.
Kish Tablet
Early Sumerian · c. 3200 BCE
A small limestone tablet from Kish covered in proto-cuneiform signs — possibly the earliest known writing. Accounts of barley, fish, and a threshing sled.
Sumerian Striding Goat
Sumer · c. 3200 BCE · limestone with Egyptian blue inlay
A limestone goat with inlaid blue eyes, from the Uruk period just before writing — Sumerian temple workshops fixed the animal forms that would echo through the next three millennia.
Mask of Warka
Uruk · c. 3100 BCE · Sumer
The first known naturalistic human face in art — a white marble mask, probably of the goddess Inanna. Looted in 2003, recovered in a farmer's garden.
Bull Procession Cup
Sumerian, Iraq · c. 3100–2900 BCE · gray limestone
Head of a Bull
Mesopotamia, Sumerian, Iraq, Tigris-Euphrates valley, Early Bronze Age I (c. 3300–c. 3000 BCE) · 3000–2000 BCE · chalcedony
Statuette of a Striding Figure
by Mesopotamian or Iranian
Iraq · proto-elamite · -3000–-2800 · Copper
Bull Calf Polemount
Sumerian, Iraq · c. 2700 BCE · basalt
Tell Asmar Hoard
Early Dynastic Sumerian · c. 2700 BCE · Eshnunna
A cache of twelve alabaster worshiper statues with oversized inlaid eyes of shell and bitumen, designed to gaze eternally at the deity. Found buried beneath the floor of the Square Temple, they stood in for their absent owners in perpetual prayer.
Standard of Ur
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE
A wooden box inlaid with lapis, shell, and red limestone. One side shows war; the other, a banquet after the victory.
Queen Puabi's Headdress
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE · Ur
Gold leaves, lapis lazuli pendants, carnelian beads, tied with ribbon hair-wire. Queen Puabi went to her grave wearing this, attended by 23 servants — also buried.
Royal Cemetery of Ur
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE · Iraq
Sixteen royal graves, each with servants, soldiers, and musicians sacrificed and buried beside the king. Out came the Standard of Ur, Queen Puabi's headdress, and the Ram in the Thicket.
Ram in a Thicket
Sumerian · c. 2500 BCE · Royal Cemetery of Ur
A goat rearing against a golden bush, made of wood, gold leaf, silver, lapis, and shell. Leonard Woolley named it for Abraham's offering in Genesis.
Lyres of Ur
Sumerian · c. 2500 BCE · Royal Cemetery
Wooden lyres covered in gold and lapis, the sound boxes shaped into bulls' heads. Found in Queen Puabi's grave next to the women who played them. The oldest stringed instruments we have.
Sumerian Libation Plaque
Early Dynastic · c. 2500 BCE · Ur
A perforated stone plaque once hung in a temple. The top register shows a naked priest pouring an offering to a seated god; the lower register, worshipers bringing livestock.
Stele of the Vultures
Sumerian · c. 2450 BCE · Lagash
The earliest known battle monument. King Eannatum's army marches in lockstep behind their shields; on the other side, vultures carry off the heads of the defeated.
Statue of Ebih-Il
Sumerian · c. 2400 BCE · Mari
An administrator from Mari, seated with hands folded in prayer, dressed in a tufted skirt. Lapis and shell eyes still stare from a white gypsum face.
Ebla Tablets
Early Bronze Age archive · c. 2350 BCE · Syria
About 17,000 cuneiform tablets found stacked on collapsed wooden shelves in the burned royal palace of Tell Mardikh. Written in an early Semitic language previously unknown, they revealed that Syria was already a literate, cosmopolitan kingdom a thousand years before Hammurabi.
Victory Stele of Sargon of Akkad
Akkadian · c. 2330 BCE · Iraq (found at Susa)
A heavily damaged limestone stele showing Sargon, the world's first empire-builder, leading bound captives while vultures pick at the fallen. Its hierarchical scaling — king monumental, enemies tiny — fixed a template that would outlast Mesopotamia by millennia.
Ebla
Early Bronze Age · c. 2500–1600 BCE · Syria
A Syrian city-state whose palace archive held 20,000 cuneiform tablets in a previously unknown Semitic language. Discovered in the 1970s — changed the textbook on the Bronze Age Levant.
Anubanini Rock Relief
Lullubi kingdom · c. 2300 BCE · Kermanshah, Iran
The victorious king Anubanini tramples a prostrate foe while the goddess Ishtar delivers captives on a leash. Carved into a vertical cliff, it inspired Darius's later Behistun monument four kilometers away.
Akkadian Head of a Ruler
Akkadian · c. 2300–2000 BCE
A heavy copper head with a beard in small ribbed curls, eyes once inlaid — one of the earliest large-scale cast-copper portraits anywhere. Nose damaged, no identification certain.
Victory Stele of Naram-Sin
Akkadian · c. 2250 BCE
The king climbs a mountain over dying enemies, horned helmet marking him as divine. The first ruler in history to have himself depicted as a god.
Head of an Akkadian Ruler
Akkadian Empire · c. 2250 BCE · Nineveh
Bronze portrait head found buried at Nineveh, likely Sargon or Naram-Sin. Eyes once set with precious stones, gouged out in antiquity as an act of symbolic regicide.
Mari Ziggurat
Early Dynastic → Old Babylonian · c. 2500–1750 BCE · Syria
A trading capital on the middle Euphrates. Its palace mural of a royal investiture is one of the oldest paintings on plaster to survive.
Head of Gudea, ensi of Lagash
Mesopotamia, Iraq, Lagash (Tello) · 2144–2124 BCE · dolerite
Statue of Gudea
Neo-Sumerian · c. 2120 BCE · Lagash
More than twenty small diorite statues of the prince of Lagash survive, each showing him at prayer. The diorite was hauled from the Sinai; carving it took tools that barely existed.
Lioness
Sumerian, Iraq · c. 2100 BCE · copper alloy, lost-wax cast
"Foundation Nail" of Gudea
Mesopotamia, Neo-Sumerian, Iraq, probably Lagash · c. 2100 BCE · copper alloy, lost-wax cast
Ziggurat of Ur
Neo-Sumerian · c. 2100 BCE · Iraq
A stepped temple-platform to the moon god Nanna. Its baked-brick facade has been partly restored; the core is as old as the pyramids.
Code of Ur-Nammu
Neo-Sumerian · c. 2100 BCE
The oldest known written law code, predating Hammurabi by three centuries. The laws are oddly lenient — mostly fines, only occasional death.
Eridu Ziggurat
Ubaid–Ur III · c. 5400–2100 BCE · southern Iraq
Thought to be the oldest city in Mesopotamia. Eighteen superimposed temple layers stand beneath the surviving ziggurat of Ur-Nammu, built over a succession of shrines to the water god Enki stretching back five thousand years.
Stele of Ur-Nammu
Ur III · c. 2100 BCE
A three-meter limestone stele showing Ur-Nammu ascending to a seated moon-god, a temple being measured and built. Smashed in antiquity; reconstructed fragment by fragment.
Statue of Gudea (Met)
Neo-Sumerian · c. 2090 BCE
One of 27 surviving statues of the ruler of Lagash, diorite hauled by camel from the Gulf. The inscription on his lap reads: "Gudea, the man who built the temple, may his life be long."
Sword Blade
Iran, Luristan · 2000–1000 BCE · bronze
Cotter Pin for Chariot
Iran, Luristan · 2000–1000 BCE · bronze
Sword Handle
Iran, Luristan · 2000–1000 BCE · bronze
Sword
Iran, Luristan · 2000–1000 BCE · bronze
Necklace or Torque
Iran, Luristan · 2000–1000 BCE · bronze
Domestic Axe-Head
Iran, Luristan · 2000–1000 BCE · bronze
Axe-Head with Four Picks
Iran, Luristan · 2000–1000 BCE · bronze
Burney Relief
Old Babylonian · c. 1800 BCE
A nude winged goddess standing on two lions, flanked by owls. Most likely Ereshkigal, queen of the underworld — or Inanna, descending into it.
Code of Hammurabi
Old Babylonian · c. 1754 BCE
Diorite stele, seven feet tall. 282 laws in cuneiform — "an eye for an eye" comes from here. Now in the Louvre.
Babylon
Old Babylonian → Neo-Babylonian · 18th c. BCE – 6th c. BCE
Capital of Hammurabi, Nebuchadnezzar, and Cyrus. Reconstructed in the 1980s by Saddam Hussein with bricks stamped in his own name.
Priest-King or Deity
Hittite, North Syria · c. 1600 BCE · basalt with bone eyes (left, ancient; right, restored)
Libation Dish-Jar Stopper
Syria, Hittite (?) or Middle Iron Age, Tell Ahmar, Djezizeh · 1600–700 BCE · stone
Lion Gate of Hattusa
Hittite · c. 1400 BCE · Turkey
Two lions carved from the same stone as the gate jambs — one of six ceremonial gates of the Hittite capital. You entered the city through their snarl.
Sphinx Gate of Alaca Höyük
Hittite · c. 1400 BCE · Turkey
A pair of sphinxes guarding the southern entrance to this Hittite sanctuary city, their hair carved into tight curls. Smaller and earlier than the lions at Hattusa — and remained in situ.
Hittite Stag Poletop
Anatolia · Hittite · 14th–12th c. BCE · bronze
A cast-bronze stag that crowned a wooden standard — the Hittites worshipped a stag-god alongside the storm-god, and metal figurines like this stood on ceremonial carts.
Hittite Relief Vessel with Ritual Scenes
Anatolia · Hittite · 14th–12th c. BCE · burnished earthenware
An earthenware jar molded in four panels of Hittite ritual scenes — one of only a handful of figurative narrative vessels from the empire that fought Ramesses II at Kadesh.
Statue of Napir-Asu
Elamite · c. 1340 BCE
Headless queen cast in solid bronze, 1.7 tons of metal. An inscription curses anyone who defaces her: may their descendants be destroyed. Threat held — the head, not the name, is gone.
Chogha Zanbil
Elamite · c. 1250 BCE · Iran
The best-preserved ziggurat in the world — five stepped stages of mud brick, built for the god Inshushinak and abandoned when the city fell.
Yazılıkaya
Hittite · 13th c. BCE · Turkey
An open-air rock sanctuary near Hattusa. Sixty-six gods and goddesses walk in procession along the cliff face — the full Hittite pantheon gathered in stone.
Hittite Seated Deity
Hittite Empire · 13th c. BCE · Anatolia
A cast bronze figure in a high conical headdress. The lost Hittite pantheon ran to thousands of names — only a handful survive in objects like this one.
Yazılıkaya Reliefs
Hittite · 13th c. BCE · Turkey
An open-air rock sanctuary above Hattusa: two natural chambers with processions of gods carved into the limestone walls. The Hittite pantheon, arranged as if on parade.
Marlik Gold
Late Bronze Age · c. 1200 BCE · Gilan, Iran
A cemetery of about fifty-three princely tombs preserved under mudslide, yielding gold beakers embossed with winged bulls, mythical griffons, and hunting scenes. The humped-bull vessels testify to a local art tradition between the Iranian plateau and the Caspian.
Amlash Lion Hunting Cup
Amlash (Iran) · 11th c. BCE · silver
A raised silver cup from south of the Caspian, showing a hunter driving his spear into a lion. Amlash metalwork surfaces in tombs along a mountain corridor Iranian-speakers followed south into the plateau.
Ibex Whetstone Handle
Iran, Luristan · 1100–900 BCE · bronze, cast
Beaker
Iran, Marlik · 1100–1000 BCE · gold, low relief repoussé and incised

1000 BCE – 0

Luristan Bronzes
Iron Age nomadic · c. 1200–700 BCE · Zagros Mountains, Iran
Thousands of cast-bronze horse bits, standard finials, and pins recovered from shaft graves in western Iran. Animals interlock with humans in tight symmetries that may record forgotten myths of horse-riding pastoralists.
Ahiram Sarcophagus
Phoenician · c. 1000 BCE · Byblos
Limestone sarcophagus of a king of Byblos. Its inscription is among the oldest substantial texts in the Phoenician alphabet — the writing system that would seed Greek, Latin, and most alphabets since.
Marlik Golden Cup
Iron Age Iran · c. 1000 BCE
A gold beaker chased with winged bulls, repeating in a frieze. From a cemetery of tribal chiefs excavated near the Caspian coast — a rare glimpse of a culture that left no writing.
Hasanlu Gold Bowl
Iron Age · c. 1000 BCE · northwestern Iran
A repoussé gold bowl found clutched by three dead men in the ruins of Hasanlu IV, burnt in an attack around 800 BCE. Scenes of gods and chariots — the style pre-Persian, still unnamed.
Votive Pin
Iran, Luristan · early 1000s BCE · bronze
Tell Halaf
Early Iron Age · 10th c. BCE · Syria
Aramean palace built of basalt orthostats — grim, angular statues of gods, scorpions, and lions. Bombed to rubble in Berlin in 1943; a team spent decades reassembling 27,000 fragments.
Hero and Animal Combat Beaker
Iran, Luristan · 900–700 BCE · silver, repoussé, engraved
Button-Based Situla
Iran, Luristan · 900–700 BCE · bronze, repoussé, punched, incised
Button-Based Situlas
Iran, Luristan · 900–700 BCE · bronze, repoussé, punched, incised
Hand Drummer
Assyrian, Iraq or possibly Syria · 900–700 BCE · bronze, solid cast
Decorative Plaque: Browsing Stag
Phoenicia, Iraq, Nimrud · 900–800 BCE · ivory
Decorative Plaque: Ram-Headed Sphinxes Flanking a Sacred Tree
Phoenicia, Iraq, Nimrud · 900–800 BCE · ivory
Saluting Protective Spirit
Neo-Assyrian (911–609 BCE), Iraq, Nimrud, Northwest Palace, reign of Ashurnasirpal II (883–859 BCE) · 883–859 BCE · gypsum
Relief Showing the Head of a Winged Genius
by Mesopotamian, Assyrian
Nimrud · late assyrian · -883–-859 · Gypsum relief with traces of pigment
Ashurnasirpal II Relief Panel
Neo-Assyrian · c. 880 BCE · Nimrud
A gypsum alabaster relief from Ashurnasirpal's Northwest Palace — the king attended by winged apkallu (sages), standard-topped trees between them. Ritual scene carved as wallpaper.
Nimrud
Neo-Assyrian · 879 BCE onward · Iraq
Ashurnasirpal II's Northwest Palace, once lined with alabaster reliefs of hunt and war. ISIS dynamited the site in 2015; much had already been moved to museums.
Assyrian Winged Genii
Neo-Assyrian · c. 870 BCE · Nimrud
Four-winged protective spirits carved in alabaster lining Ashurnasirpal II's palace. They lift a pine cone and a bucket — a gesture of ritual purification never fully decoded.
Relief of Ashurnasirpal II with Attendant
Neo-Assyrian · c. 865 BCE · Nimrud
Alabaster wall panels from the throne room of Ashurnasirpal's northwest palace, showing the king under a divine winged sun while an attendant purifies him with a fir cone. The minutely patterned beards and tasseled robes remained the template for Assyrian royal art for two centuries.
Nimrud Ivories
Neo-Assyrian · 9th–7th c. BCE · Iraq
Thousands of carved ivory plaques — Egyptian, Phoenician, and native Assyrian styles — looted from the palace of Kalhu. Many recovered charred from a well; some destroyed by ISIS in 2015.
Balawat Gates
Neo-Assyrian · 9th c. BCE
Bronze bands that once wrapped the 7-meter-high wooden doors of Shalmaneser III's palace — embossed with tiny scenes of his campaigns. The wood rotted, the bands survived; now reassembled in the British Museum.
Phoenician Ivory: Man and Griffin
Phoenician · 9th c. BCE · Nimrud
An ivory plaque of a man combating a griffin — once inlaid in royal furniture in Phoenician cities, looted by Assyrians, buried in the wells of Nimrud as Nineveh fell.
Phoenician Ivory: Cow and Calf
Phoenician · 9th c. BCE · Nimrud
A maternal scene carved into ivory — a cow looking back as her calf nurses. Phoenician carvers worked with a Egyptian eye for landscape, an Near Eastern line.
Phoenician Ivory: Winged Sphinx
Phoenician · 9th c. BCE · Nimrud
A crouching winged sphinx ivory plaque — hybrid mythic form that Phoenician artists traded across the ancient Mediterranean. The sphinxes look Egyptian; their wings, Assyrian; their wedding, Phoenician.
Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III
Neo-Assyrian · c. 825 BCE
A tribute list in stone — foreign kings bringing gifts to Shalmaneser. One is Jehu of Israel, bowing to kiss the ground. The earliest known image of an Israelite.
Kul-e Farah Reliefs
Neo-Elamite · c. 1000–650 BCE · Izeh, Iran
Six rock-cut panels deep in a southwestern Iranian gorge showing Elamite rulers and banqueters in long processional sacrifices. Little known outside specialist circles, they are the best surviving witness to the visual culture of Elam after the Middle Elamite collapse.
Assyrian Ivory Plaque with Oryx
Neo-Assyrian · 9th–8th c. BCE
An openwork ivory plaque — an oryx grazing in a stylized forest, once set as furniture inlay. Phoenician-carved; looted by Assyrians and buried at Nimrud.
Horse Bit with Winged Ibex Cheekpieces
Iran, Luristan · 800–600 BCE · bronze, cast and incised
Votive Pin with Decorated Disc
Iran, Luristan · 800–600 BCE · silver, repoussé and incised
Axel Shaft Cup
Iran, Luristan · c. 800–700 BCE · bronze, cast
Three-Part Pitcher
Iran, Luristan · c. 800–700 BCE · earthenware, unglazed slip
Banquet Pyxis
Anatolia, Neo-Hittite · 800–750 BCE · steatite
Lamassu
Neo-Assyrian · 9th–7th c. BCE
Winged bulls with human heads, guarding palace gates at Nineveh and Nimrud. Carved with five legs so they look correct from front and side.
Luristan Horned Animal Plaque
Luristan · 8th–7th c. BCE · Iran
A bronze plaque of a stylized horned beast, part of the distinctive animal metalwork of the Luristan tribes — nomadic mountain dwellers with no cities but a rich bronze tradition.
Luristan Disc-Headed Pin
Luristan · 8th–7th c. BCE · Iran
Bronze boss from a large ceremonial pin — a stylized face or sun with radiating ornament. Luristan pins were votive offerings, deposited by the thousands at shrine sites.
Master of Animals Cheekpiece
Iran, Luristan · c. 700 BCE · bronze, cast and incised
Dur-Sharrukin (Khorsabad)
Neo-Assyrian · Sargon II · 713–706 BCE
The capital Sargon II built from scratch and moved into — only to be killed on campaign and abandoned by his heir. Its 40-ton alabaster lamassus now guard the Louvre and the Oriental Institute.
Lamassu of Khorsabad
c. 713–706 BCE · Gypsum alabaster · Richelieu Wing
Human-headed winged bulls, each over 4 meters tall. They guarded the gates of Sargon II's palace in what is now northern Iraq. Carved with five legs so that they appear to be standing still when seen from the front, and striding forward when seen from the side.
Nineveh
Neo-Assyrian capital · 7th century BCE · northern Iraq
Sennacherib's 'Palace Without Rival' and Ashurbanipal's library stood within twelve kilometers of walls. The Mashki Gate was intentionally dynamited by ISIS in 2016 and has since been partially reconstructed with its original stones.
Lachish Reliefs
Neo-Assyrian · c. 700 BCE
Gypsum slabs from Sennacherib's palace at Nineveh, documenting his siege of the Judean city of Lachish — siege engines, archers, impaled defenders, prisoners marched into exile. Ancient war journalism.
Scythian Lion Plaque
Scytho-Iranian · 1000–500 BCE
A small bronze lion plaque — the "lion in attack" pose, angular and compact. Similar forms span the steppe from Iran to Mongolia in the Iron Age.
Ibex Standard Finial
Iran, Luristan · c. 700–600 BCE · bronze
Ziwiye Hoard
Scythian/Median · 7th century BCE · Kurdistan, Iran
A spectacular cache of gold, silver, and ivory found in a bronze coffin in 1947 — or so the story goes; much of it was dispersed on the black market. Surviving pieces hybridize Scythian animal style with Assyrian and Urartian imagery.
Ziwiye Treasure
Median / Mannaean · 7th c. BCE · Iran
Gold, silver, and ivory found (or said to have been found) in a bronze coffin at Ziwiye in 1947. Scythian and Assyrian styles alongside — how much is authentic, scholars still argue.
Luristan Bull Jug
Luristan · 7th–6th c. BCE · Iran
A painted ceramic jug shaped as a recumbent bull, spout rising from its back. A domestic vessel that doubled as an animal sculpture — typical of Iron Age Iranian ceramic play.
Urartian Bull-Head Attachment
Urartu · 7th c. BCE · eastern Anatolia
A bronze bull-head fitting — the clamp for a cauldron rim, or the base of a throne leg. Urartu, the lost Iron Age kingdom around Lake Van, specialized in bronze animal protomes.
Lion Hunt of Ashurbanipal
Neo-Assyrian · c. 645 BCE · Nineveh
Alabaster reliefs from the king's palace. A dying lioness drags paralyzed hind legs — one of the most unnervingly precise animal studies in ancient art.
Banquet of Ashurbanipal
Neo-Assyrian · c. 645 BCE · Nineveh
The king reclines in a garden with his queen, a harpist playing — and the severed head of the Elamite king Teumman hangs from a tree branch beside them. Domestic scene as total war.
Median Ram-Head Rhyton
Median · c. 600 BCE · Iran
A silver ram-head drinking horn from Median Iran — predecessors to the Achaemenids. Medes dominated the Zagros before Cyrus the Great absorbed them into a larger empire.
Ishtar Gate
Neo-Babylonian · c. 575 BCE
Glazed brick gate to Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar II. Dragons and bulls in cobalt blue. Reconstructed in Berlin's Pergamon Museum.
Cyrus Cylinder
Achaemenid Persia · c. 539 BCE
A clay cylinder inscribed after Cyrus took Babylon. Often called the first charter of human rights — a claim the British Museum treats with more caution.
Tomb of Cyrus at Pasargadae
Achaemenid Persia · c. 530 BCE · Iran
A simple stepped stone tomb for the founder of the empire. Alexander, visiting 200 years later, found it looted and ordered it restored.
Ibex-Head Ornament
Iran, Achaemenid (550–330 BCE) · c. 525–450 BCE · bronze
Behistun Inscription
Achaemenid Persia · 520 BCE · Iran
Darius the Great's autobiography carved three hundred feet up a cliff, in three languages. The trilingual key that let Rawlinson decipher cuneiform.
Persepolis
Achaemenid Persia · 518 BCE onward · Iran
Ceremonial capital of Darius and Xerxes. Reliefs show envoys from every corner of the empire bringing tribute. Burned by Alexander in 330 BCE.
Frieze of the Archers of Darius I
c. 510 BCE · Glazed brick · Richelieu Wing
From the palace of Darius the Great at Susa. A procession of the king's elite guard — the "Immortals" — in brilliantly colored glazed brick. Each carries a spear, bow, and quiver; each face is subtly different from the next.
Apadana Reliefs
Achaemenid Persia · c. 500 BCE · Persepolis
The great staircase of the audience hall. Subject peoples from 23 nations process forward with tribute — Ethiopians with ivory, Lydians with bowls, Bactrian camels.
Bes-Head Necklace
Achaemenid · 6th–4th c. BCE
A gold necklace of linked beads and a central pendant shaped like the head of Bes — the grotesque Egyptian household god, picked up and recirculated through the Persian empire.
Ibex Bracelet Terminal
Iran, Achaemenid (550–330 BCE) · 500–400 BCE · gold, solid cast
Median Lion Strangler
Iran, Achaemenid (550–330 BCE) · 500–450 BCE · lapis lazuli
Lion's Head
Iran, Achaemenid (550–330 BCE) · 500s BCE · marble
Fragment of a Wall Decoration from the Palace of Xerxes: "Guardsman" in Procession
Iran, Achaemenid (550–330 BCE), Persepolis · 486–465 BCE · gray limestone
Naqsh-e Rustam
Achaemenid & Sasanian · 5th c. BCE – 3rd c. CE · Iran
Cruciform tombs cut high into a cliff for Darius the Great and his heirs. Centuries later the Sasanians carved their own triumphs below.
Achaemenid Lion Rhyton
Achaemenid · c. 5th c. BCE
A silver drinking horn terminating in the forequarters of a snarling lion with wings — ceremonial tableware for a Persian noble's banquet, found in the graves of Lydia.
Head
Persepolis · 450 BC
Achaemenid Gold Rhyton
Achaemenid Persia · 550–330 BCE
A drinking horn that ends in the forequarters of a roaring lion, carved from a single piece of gold. Ceremonial, not functional — elite tableware as declaration of empire.
Oxus Treasure
Achaemenid Persia · 5th–4th c. BCE · Tajikistan
180 gold and silver objects washed out of a riverbed — chariots, lions, votive plaques. The British Museum's greatest hoard of Achaemenid metalwork.
Pazyryk Carpet
Scytho-Achaemenid · 5th c. BCE
The oldest surviving pile carpet in the world, buried in a Scythian chieftain's frozen tomb in the Altai. Horses and deer in rows — nomadic Central Asia meeting Achaemenid design.
Scythian Elk Fawn Finial
Scythian · 4th c. BCE
A small bronze elk-fawn standard top, ears erect — Scythian nomad metalwork from the Pontic steppe. The animal style dominated Scythian visual culture from the Black Sea to Altai.
Scythian Stag Plaque
Scythian · 4th c. BCE
A cast bronze stag curled in the Scythian "sleeping deer" pose — legs tucked under, antlers sweeping back along the body. A portable ornament, sewn onto leather or horse gear.
Hedgehog Jewelry Element
Western Asia, Scythian · 400–200 BCE · gold, soldered or fused with granulation
Stater: Baal (obverse); Head of Pharnabazos (reverse)
Iran, Achaemenid (550–330 BCE), minted at Tarsos (Kilikia) · 379–374 BCE · silver
Stater: Ba'al, Seated with Scepter, within Circle (obverse)
Iran, Achaemenid (550–330 BCE), minted at Tarsos (Kilikia) · 379–374 BCE · silver
Stater: Head of Pharnabazus, Helmeted, within Circle (reverse)
Iran, Achaemenid (550–330 BCE), minted at Tarsos (Kilikia) · 379–374 BCE · silver
Man's face
Iran · 350 BC
Nisa Rhytons
Parthian · 2nd c. BCE · Turkmenistan
Carved ivory drinking horns from the Parthian royal residence at Old Nisa, ending in centaurs, griffins, or winged lions. Greek form, Parthian ritual, Central Asian material.
Drachm (Coin) Portraying King Mithridates II the Great of Parthia
by Parthian
Khorasan · parthian · -123–-88 · Silver
Denarius (Coin) Depicting the God Quirinus
by Roman, minted in Rome
Rome · parthian · -60 · Silver
Drachm (Coin) Portraying King Orodes I
by Persian; Parthia
Khorasan · parthian · -57–-37 · Silver
Drachm (Coin) Portraying King Phraates IV
by Persian; Parthia
Khorasan · parthian · -38–-2 · Silver
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying King Phraates IV
by Parthian
Iran · -38–-3 · Silver

0 – 500 CE

Petra — Al-Khazneh
Nabataean · 1st c. BCE – 1st c. CE · Jordan
The "Treasury" — a 40-meter temple-facade carved directly into the rose-red sandstone cliff. Bedouin shot at the urn at the top, hoping to spill hidden pharaoh's gold.
Temple of Anahita at Kangavar
Parthian · 2nd century BCE–2nd century CE · western Iran
A vast rectangular terrace edged with columns carved in a hybrid Greco-Persian style, dedicated to the water goddess Anahita. Its scale — more than 200 meters long — and hellenistic detailing testify to the Parthian synthesis after Alexander.
Bactrian Gold
Kushan era · 1st c. BCE–1st c. CE · Afghanistan
20,000 gold artefacts from six nomad graves at Tillya Tepe — Greek and Steppe and Indian motifs fused. Thought lost during the Soviet war; reappeared intact from a vault under the Central Bank in 2003.
Dish
Iran · 9th century-10th century
Ruins at Palmyra, Syria
Syria · late 19th century
Cylinder seal
Mesopotamia · c. 20th century bc - c. 18th century BC
Ewer
Iran · 7th century
Tethering-Ring
Iran · 13th century
Indigo crucible
Iran · 11th century
Bowl-Stand
Iran · 12th century
mirror (ayine)
Iran · 10th century
Dish fragment
Iran · 15th century
Parthian Head of a Girl
Parthian · 1st–2nd c. CE · terracotta
A small terracotta head, broken from a figurine — mass-produced in Parthian workshops between Tigris and Euphrates, where Greek portrait conventions met Iranian faces.
Peacock Ring
Seleucia, Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · gold and chalcedony
Pendant Earring
Seleucia, Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · gold with garnet and pearls
Pendant or Earring
Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · gold
Draped Figure
Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · terracotta

Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · terracotta

Vase

Figurine
Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · bone
Head of a Weeping Woman
Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · terracotta
Garnet
Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · 1–200 CE · gold and garnet

Drachm (Coin) Portraying King Gotarzes II
by Persian; Parthia
Khorasan · parthian · 40–51 · Silver
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying King Gotarzes
by Parthian
Iran · 40–51 · Silver
Feline-Handled Incense Burner
Burudjird(?), Parthian Empire (247 BCE–224 CE) · c. 100 CE · bronze, cast
Palmyrene Procession of Nobles
Palmyra · 100–150 CE · limestone
A relief of robed Palmyrene nobles in frontal procession — the caravan city brokered silk between Rome and Parthia, before Zenobia's revolt brought Aurelian's legions down on it in 272.
Drachm (Coin) Portraying King Mithradates IV
by Persian; Parthia
Khorasan · parthian · 130–147 · Silver
Coin Portraying Emperor Hadrian
by Roman, minted in Alexandria, Egypt
Khorasan · parthian · 133–134 · Bronze
Hatra
Parthian · 1st–3rd c. CE · Iraq
A desert caravan city whose kings commissioned limestone statues fusing Hellenistic drapery with Parthian dress. Destroyed by ISIS in 2015.
Tang-e Sarvak Reliefs
Elymaid · 2nd c. CE · Iran
Rock reliefs in a remote Zagros gorge — kings on horseback, banquet scenes, in a provincial Parthian style that ignores every classical proportion and still somehow works.
Tetradrachm (Coin) Portraying Bust of King Volagases IV
by Parthian
Iran · 192–193 · Silver
Horse-Shaped Drinking Vessel
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 200–325 CE · silver, partially gilt
Jar
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 222–650 CE · earthenware
Rim Fragment
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 222–650 CE · earthenware
Silver Dish
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 226–641 CE · silver
Dura-Europos Frescoes
Parthian · 1st–3rd c. CE · Syria
A caravan-city on the Euphrates whose buried buildings preserved the earliest synagogue murals, the earliest Christian house-church paintings, and Greek, Palmyrene, and Mithraic frescoes — all within blocks of each other.
Tomb Relief
Syria, Palmyra · 230 CE · limestone
Naqsh-e Rajab
Sasanian · 3rd c. CE · Iran
Rock reliefs near Persepolis celebrating the founder of the Sasanian empire. Ardashir receives the crown directly from Ahura Mazda — politics cast as theology.
Colossus of Shapur I
Sasanian · 3rd century CE · Bishapur cave, Iran
A seven-meter standing figure carved from a stalagmite deep in a cave above Bishapur. Shapur stands in full regalia, his thick beard curled and mustaches pointing sideways in the signature Sasanian style; fragments of his arms were reassembled in the twentieth century.
Bishapur
Sasanian royal city · 266 CE · Fars, Iran
Built by Shapur I to commemorate his victory over three Roman emperors, including the capture of Valerian. Its grid plan drew on Antioch; its palace mosaics were pure Roman workmanship executed by prisoners-of-war.
Anahita Vessel
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 300–500 CE · silver, cast, raised, repoussé, chased, and gilted
Drachm: Fire altar with bust of Hormizd II, Zoroastrian priest holding sword left, King as priest holding sword right (reverse)
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · 303–9 CE · silver
Drachm: Bust of Hormizd II (obverse); Fire Altar with Bust (reverse)
Iran, (minted), Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · 302–9 CE · silver
Drachm: Fire Altar with Bust, Zoroastrian Priest and King as Priest (reverse)
Iran, (minted), Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · 302–9 CE · silver
Drachm: Bust of Hormizd II, r., wearing crown (obverse)
Iran, (minted), Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · 303–9 CE · silver
Drachm: Bust of Hormizd II Wearing Crown (obverse)
Iran, (minted), Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · 302–9 CE · silver
Drachm: Fire altar with bust, Zoroastrian priest holding sword left, King as priest holding sword right (reverse)
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · 302–309 CE · silver
Drachma: Bust of Hormizd II, r., wearing crown (obverse)
Iran, (minted), Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · 302–9 CE · silver
Coin Portraying King Sapor II
by Sasanian; Persia
Khorasan · parthian · 309–379 · Silver
Sasanian Royal Head
Sasanian · 4th c. CE
A silver-gilt head, perhaps of Shapur II — crown covered in stamped beading, eyes huge and ringed. Sasanian royal portraiture preserved the Achaemenid taste for frontal hierarchy.
Sasanian King Hunting Lions
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · late 300s CE · alabaster
Ibex Eating
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · c. 400–600 CE · stucco (gypsum plaster) with traces of paint
Fire Altar Intaglio
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE), reign of Hormizd II (303–309) · c. 400 CE · chalcedony
Taq Kasra
Sasanian · 3rd–6th c. CE · Iraq
The largest single-span brick vault in the ancient world — the throne hall of the Sasanian palace at Ctesiphon. It still stands, barely.
Sasanian Royal Hunt Dish
Sasanian Iran · 5th–6th c. CE · silver gilt
King Hormizd on horseback twisting to shoot a lion that has leapt onto his saddle. Sasanian hunt dishes travelled as royal diplomatic gifts, reaching as far as the Volga.
Stepped Pitcher
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 400–600 CE · silver
Dish: The Goddess Anahita
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 400–600 CE · silver gilt

500 – 1000

Taq-e Bostan
Sasanian · 4th–7th century CE · Kermanshah, Iran
Rock-cut grottoes carved into a cliff at a sacred spring, showing Sasanian kings receiving divine investiture and hunting boar from reed-filled marshes. The larger grotto's royal boar-hunt panel is among the most elaborate narrative reliefs in Iranian art.
Sasanian Water Buffalo Rhyton
Sasanian Iran · 6th–7th c. · gilt silver with glass inlay
A drinking horn whose spout is a water buffalo, its rider a woman — likely the goddess Anahita, whose cult fused fertility, water, and war in late Sasanian Iran.
Bowl
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 500–700 CE · silver
Hunt Pitcher
Iran, Sasanian Empire (224–651 CE) · 500–600 · silver
Ladle
Syria(?), Achaemenid (550–330 BCE) · c. 525–450 · silver
Drachma (Coin) Portraying Chosroes I
by Sasanian; Persia
Iran · sassanian · 531–579 · Silver
Sasanian Silver Plate
Sasanian · 5th–7th c. CE
Hammered silver with gilded highlights — a king hunting lions, or a noble in ceremonial costume on horseback. Sasanian court plates traveled from Iran as far as Viking Scandinavia.
Woven silk
Iran · 700-900
Storage jar
Iran · 750-1000
Bowl with Pseudo Inscription
by Iran
Iran · 899–999 · Earthenware painted in manganese purple with splashes of green on an opaque white glaze
Nishapur Figural Bowl
Iran, Nishapur, Samanid period (819–1005) · 900s · Earthenware with underglaze decoration
Bowl with Two Birds
Iran, Nishapur or Central Asia, Samarkand, Samanid Period, 10th century · 900s · earthenware with underglaze slip-painted decoration
Tombstone
Iran, Nishapur, 10th century · 900s · limestone
Bowl with Incised Decoration
by Iran
Iran · amol · 900–1099 · Earthenware with incised decorations and polychrome painting under transparent glaze
Bowl with Incised and Splashed Decoration
by Iran
Iran · 901–1000 · Earthenware with incised decorations and splashed with green and brown under transparent glaze

1000 – 1500

by Eastern Turkey or Western Iran
